coredata.dc:description: The development of bacterial resistance significantly contributes to the persistence of infections. Although previous studies have highlighted the benefits of metal-doped positive carbon nanodots in managing bacterial wound infections, their mechanism of action is relatively simple and they may pose potential hazards to human cells. Therefore, it is essential to develop a one-stop carbon dot nanoplatform that offers high biocompatibility, antibacterial properties, and anti-inflammatory activities for wound infection management. This study explores the antibacterial efficacy, without detectable resistance, and wound-healing potential of nitrogen-doped (N-doped) negatively charged carbon dots (TPP-CDs). These carbon dots are synthesized using tannic acid (TA), polyethylene polyamine, and polyethylene glycol (PEG) as precursors, with a focus on their biocompatibility. Numerous systematic studies have shown that TPP-CDs can effectively destroy bacterial biofilms and deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), while also inducing oxidative stress, leading to a potent antimicrobial effect. TPP-CDs also demonstrate the ability to scavenge excess free radicals, promote cellular proliferation, and inhibit inflammatory factors, all of which contribute to improved wound healing. TPP-CDs also demonstrate favorable cell imaging capabilities. These findings suggest that N-doped negatively charged TPP-CDs hold significant potential for treating bacterial infections and offer practical insights for their application in the medical field.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of TPP-CDs. (B) TEM and inset size distribution images of TPP-CDs. Scale bar: 50 nm. (C) FTIR spectra of TPP-CDs. (D) High-resolution XPS survey spectra of TPP-CDs, (E) C1s, (F) N1s, (G) O1s. (H) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CDs (inset shows TPP-CDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light and corresponding the Tyndall effect). (I) Optimal excitation and emission spectra of TPP-CDs. (J, K) Fluorescence spectra of TPP-CDs excited at different wavelengths.

In vitro antibacterial characteristics of TPP-CDs. (A) Images of S. aureus and E. coli treated with the same concentration of TA and TPP-CDs (800 μg·mL−1). (B) Images of S. aureus and E. coli treated with different concentrations of TPP-CDs (0, 100, 200, 400, 800 and 1600 μg·mL−1). (C) Viability of bacteria of S. aureus and E. coli treated with TA and TPP-CDs. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (D) Viability of bacteria of S. aureus and E. coli treated with different concentrations of TPP-CDs (100, 200, 400, 800 and 1600 μg·mL−1). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (E) The growth curves of S. aureus and (F) E. coli treated with TA and different concentrations of TPP-CDs (0 μg·mL−1, 1/2MIC, MIC, 2MIC, MBC). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (G) Studies on the emergence of S. aureus and (H) E. coli resistance to TPP-CDs and amoxicillin. (I) Images representing live/dead bacterial staining fluorescence and flow cytometry in different treatment groups with varying TPP-CDs concentration (0, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1). Scale bar: 20 µm. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (J) Histograms representing S. aureus and (K) E. coli treated with different concentrations of TPP-CDs (0, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1).

(A) Crystal violet staining images from groups treated with different concentrations of TPP-CDs (0, 200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1) and biofilm microscopic images at 800 μg·mL−1. Scale bar: 200 µm. (B) Quantitative analysis of biofilm clearance. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. Fluorescence staining images (C) and quantitative analysis (D) of ROS in S. aureus and E. coli in different treatment groups (0, 200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1). Scale bar: 10 µm. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (E) Typical SEM images of S. aureus and E. coli untreated and treated with TPP-CDs. The red box indicates the bacterial membrane damage fraction. Scale bar: 5 µm. (F) Agarose gel electrophoresis of DNA from S. aureus and E. coli treated with TPP-CDs (0, 200, and 400 μg·mL−1). (G) Schematic representation of membrane damage. (*p < 0.05, ***p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cell viability of (A) HUVEC, (B) L929, and (C) RAW264.7 incubated with different concentrations of TPP-CDs (0, 12.25, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, and 1600 μg·mL−1). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. Representative microscopic images of scratch healing for HUVEC cells (D) and L929 cells (F) after treatment with TPP-CDs for 12, 24, and 36 h. Scale bar: 100 µm. (E, G) Statistical analysis of (D, F). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (H) ABTS and (I) DPPH scavenging rate of CDs. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (J) Quantitative analysis of relative expression level of related genes (TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6) in Lps-induced RAW264.7 cells treated with TPP-CDs. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (K) Western blotting of TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 in LPS-induced RAW264.7 cells treated with TPP-CDs (0, 200,and 400 μg·mL−1). (L) Quantitative analysis of TNF-α, IL-1β and IL-6 expression in LPS-induced RAW264.7 cells treated with TPP-CDs (0, 200,and 400 μg·mL−1). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (M) Schematic diagram representing wound healing. (***p < 0.001).

In vivo antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and wound healing potential of TPP-CDs. (A) Schematic diagram representing the establishment of infected wound model and representative photographs of wound healing in mice treated with the indicated treatment from day 0 to day 10. (B) Schematic diagram of wound shrinkage after TPP-CDs treatment from day 0 to day 10. (C) Relative wound area of mice. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (D) Photographs of bacterial colonies formed by S. aureus in wound tissue on days 0, 4, and 8. (E) Bacterial viability was quantified at the wound site on days 4 and (F) 8. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (G) H&E and Masson staining of wound tissue. Left scale bar: 100 µm; middle scale bar: 50 μm; right scale bar: 50 µm. The middle pictures are the magnified views of the black dashed boxes in the left picture. Blue arrows indicate fibroblasts, yellow arrows indicate inflammatory cells, red arrows indicate angiogenesis, and black arrows indicate hair follicles. (H) Quantitative analysis of epidermal thickness. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (I) Quantitative analysis of collagen deposition. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (J) Immunohistochemical staining for IL-6, TNF-α, and VEGF. Scale bar: 100 µm. Quantitative analysis of IL-6 (K), TNF-α (L), and VEGF (M) positive cells in the wound tissue of each group. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 6. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Safety evaluation of TPP-CDs. (A) Hemolysis rate following treatment with TPP-CDs at different concentrations (200, 400, 800, 1600, and 3200 μg·mL−1). Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3. (B) H&E staining was used to evaluate the histopathological effects of TPP-CDs on the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) in mice. Scale bar: 50 μm. Effect of TPP-CDs on blood routine examination such as HGB (C), Granin # (D), WBC (E), and PLT(F) in mice. Data are presented as mean ± SD, n = 3.
Schematic illustration of TPP-CDs preparation and their multifunctional application in wound healing.

head.ce:title: Construction of a one-stop N-doped negatively charged carbon dot nanoplatform with antibacterial and anti-inflammatory dual activities for wound infection based on biocompatibility

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of bacterial resistance significantly contributes to the persistence of infections. Although previous studies have highlighted the benefits of metal-doped positive carbon nanodots in managing bacterial wound infections, their mechanism of action is relatively simple and they may pose potential hazards to human cells. Therefore, it is essential to develop a one-stop carbon dot nanoplatform that offers high biocompatibility, antibacterial properties, and anti-inflammatory activities for wound infection management. This study explores the antibacterial efficacy, without detectable resistance, and wound-healing potential of nitrogen-doped (N-doped) negatively charged carbon dots (TPP-CDs). These carbon dots are synthesized using tannic acid (TA), polyethylene polyamine, and polyethylene glycol (PEG) as precursors, with a focus on their biocompatibility. Numerous systematic studies have shown that TPP-CDs can effectively destroy bacterial biofilms and deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), while also inducing oxidative stress, leading to a potent antimicrobial effect. TPP-CDs also demonstrate the ability to scavenge excess free radicals, promote cellular proliferation, and inhibit inflammatory factors, all of which contribute to improved wound healing. TPP-CDs also demonstrate favorable cell imaging capabilities. These findings suggest that N-doped negatively charged TPP-CDs hold significant potential for treating bacterial infections and offer practical insights for their application in the medical field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections have long posed a serious threat to human life and health. A 2022 report published in The Lancet highlighted the global impact and reported that these infections were responsible for more than one million deaths worldwide [1]. The skin serves as the body's primary protective barrier, directly exposed to external elements and highly susceptible to damage from environmental factors. When the skin is injured, bacteria can invade the wound, impeding the healing process, causing local infections, exacerbating complications, and in severe cases, leading to mortality [2,3].

Antibiotics are commonly used in clinical settings to treat bacterial infections in wounds. However, the misuse of antibiotics, bacterial enzyme resistance to antibacterial therapeutics, and the formation of biofilms have contributed to the emergence of multiple drug-resistant bacterial strains [4]. This resistance intensifies the inflammatory response within wounds and obstructs the natural healing process [5]. Consequently, there is a critical need for the development of novel antibacterial agents.

The advancement of nanotechnology has enabled the synthesis of various nanomaterials, including metal–organic frameworks, nanoenzymes, antimicrobial peptides, and similar materials [6]. These nanomaterials offer significant advantages in treating bacterial infections due to their extremely small size, which increases the contact area with microorganisms and reduces the likelihood of the development of resistance through multiple mechanisms [7]. However, metallic nanoparticles may pose risks of biological toxicity and metabolic issues due to the release of metallic compounds. Similarly, nanoenzymes, antimicrobial peptides, and other materials face challenges such as high costs, complex catalytic parameters, and intricate synthesis processes. The photoluminescent properties of carbon dots (CDs), which are carbon nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm, have attracted considerable interest [8]. These nanoparticles represent a novel form of carbon-based material characterized by zero dimensions [9–11]. They can be readily prepared at low cost and demonstrate outstanding biocompatibility, water solubility, and abundance of surface-active groups [12,13]. CDs have emerged as a promising alternative for treating drug-resistant microbes [14–16]. CDs interact with bacteria through electrostatic adsorption [17,18], enabling them to remove bacterial biofilms or induce oxidative stress, ultimately disrupting bacterial structures. Among them, most are metal-doped positively charged CDs [19]. Wang et al. [20] fabricated positively charged silver-doped carbon dots (CDs) that accumulate on the surface of bacteria, triggering the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which subsequently kill the bacteria. In a separate study, Wang et al. [21] developed iron-doped CDs that, when combined with photothermal therapy, effectively eliminate bacterial biofilms, providing significant therapeutic benefits. Due to the presence of unpaired electrons, CDs can scavenge excess free radicals from wounds, promoting wound healing [22,23]. Additionally, Qu et al. [24] created positively charged CDs that exhibit both antibacterial and antioxidant properties. However, the positive charge of certain CDs, while effective against bacteria, may also pose a risk to normal human cells due to their negative charge. In contrast, negatively charged CDs offer greater safety and are more easily able to penetrate cell membranes [25,26]. For example, the negatively charged guanosine-derived CDs prepared by Nong et al. [27] exhibit antibacterial effects by disrupting the bacterial respiratory chain and reducing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) levels. Similarly, the negatively charged tetramethylpyrazine CDs developed by Zhu et al. [28] treat bacterial-infected wounds by inhibiting bacterial DNA repair, increasing ROS production, and promoting coagulation. However, the primary function of these CDs is to destroy bacteria, with a relatively simple mechanism, and only a few multifunctional CDs also exhibit anti-inflammatory effects that promote wound healing. Therefore, it is crucial to develop one-stop carbon dot nanoplatforms that offer high biocompatibility, along with both antibacterial and anti-inflammatory activities, to effectively address the treatment of wound infections.

This study utilizes an innovative approach for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped (N-doped) negatively charged carbon dots (TPP-CDs) using tannic acid (TA), polyethylene polyamine, and polyethylene glycol (PEG). The preparation is simple and efficient. Based on their favorable biocompatibility and optical properties, TPP-CDs have demonstrated the ability to destroy bacterial biofilms and DNA, while inducing bacteria to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), resulting in broad-spectrum antibacterial effects. TPP-CDs also exhibit excellent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities alongside their antibacterial action, significantly accelerating wound healing. This N-doped, negatively charged TPP-CDs nanoplatform, with its highly efficient antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, holds significant promise for advancing the treatment of bacterial wound infections and the development of innovative antibacterial nanomaterials. (See Scheme 1 ).

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 6538) and escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) were purchased from Beijing microbiological culture collection center (Beijing China). Tannic acid (TA, 98 %) was purchased from Shanghai Rhawn Technology Co., LTD. Polyethylene polyamine (Nitrogen content 33.6 %) and crystal violet dye (ACS, 90 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mn1500) was purchased from Beijing Mreda Technology Co., LTD. Sodium chloride (NaCl, AR, 99.5 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, 96 %) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., LTD. Beef extract, agar powder and sephadex G-50 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Peptone was purchased from Beijing Abxing Biotechnology Co., LTD. Live and dead bacteria staining kit-N01/propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Beijing Baiao Leibo Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified eagle media (DMEM) was purchased from Jiangsu Keygentec Biotechnology Co., LTD. 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), pancreatic enzymes, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) were purchased from Shanghai Beyotime Technology Co., LTD. Methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98 %) was purchased from Beijing Labgic Co., LTD. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Hongkong Eva Holdings Co., LTD.

0.05 g TA was weighed and dissolved in 60 ml ultrapure water. Then, 0.63 ml of polyethylene polyamine were added. Additionally, 0.57 g PEG of different molecular weights (400, 800, and 1500) were added or no PEG was added at all. Following ultrasonic dissolution, The mixture was reacted at 180 °C for 6 h in a reaction kettle. And the solid substance was separated from the liquid by spinning it at a speed of 12,000 rpm for 10 min. Using 1.5 kDa dialysis bags or sephadex G-50 column purified TPP-CDs. It was then freeze-dried to get solid TPP-CDs with different molecular weights PEG. These solid powders were then stored in a dark dry environment.

The morphology of TPP-CDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7800, Hitachi, Japan), the particle size distribution and Zeta-potential (Z-potential) of TPP-CDs were measured using a Malvern Nano ZS90 particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). The chemical composition of TPP-CDs was determined through analysis utilizing a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (TENSOR27T, Bruker, Germany). The ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectra of the samples were acquired using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer manufactured by Jinghua in Shanghai. A Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrophotometer was used to collect fluorescence spectra. A Shimadzu AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was used to acquire X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of TPP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The frozen S. aureus and E. coli were transferred from the refrigerator at −80 °C to luria–bertani (LB) liquid media for inoculation. The bacteria were placed in a shaker and incubated them overnight at a temperature of 37 °C. After two generations, the diluted bacterial solution (1 × 106 CFU·mL−1) and the same concentration of TA and TPP-CDs (800 μg·mL−1) were incubated together for 3 h. 100 μL of the sample to be testde distributed onto a solid media overnight at a temperature of 37 °C. After that, we photographed and counted the media.

The study assessed the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC), and bacterial resistance of TPP-CDs [29]. TPP-CDs of different concentrations (200, 400, 800 and 1600 μg·mL−1) were prepared with LB media. The above solutions were then mixed with bacterial suspensions (1 × 106 CFU·mL−1) at a 37 °C incubator for 18 h. Bacterial proliferation was observed, and the absorbance at a wavelength of 600 nm was quantified to determine the MIC. 100 μL bacterial solution distributed onto solid media overnight to determine the MBC. Bacterial suspensions (1 × 106 CFU·mL−1) at a 1/2MIC concentration were cultivated in LB media supplemented with TPP-CDs and amoxicillin. The MIC was calculated again using the same method as before. The culture was then passed 15 times, and any changes in MIC were noted.

Turbidimetry was performed to determine the effectiveness of TPP-CDs in inhibiting S. aureus and E. coli growth. After the bacteria were cultured, TPP-CDs of different concentrations (200, 400, 800 and 1600 μg·mL−1) were prepared with LB media. The bacteria (1 × 106 CFU·mL−1) were inoculated into a culture media containing TPP-CDs and placed in an incubator set at 37 °C. Optical density (OD, 600 nm) was collected at different time intervals, and a graphical representation was generated to depict the collected data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent staining and Quantitative sorting of Live/Dead bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: N01 and PI used as a probe to detect the detect live/dead bacteria. N01 was excited at a wavelength of 488 nm and emitted light at 525 nm, the PI was excited at 535 nm and emitted light at 617 nm [19]. As previously mentioned, bacteria (1 × 108 CFU·mL−1) were incubated with TPP-CDs at different concentrations (400 μg·mL−1 and 800 μg·mL−1) which were prepared with LB media for a duration of 3 h. The sample to be tested was washed twice with PBS (centrifugation of 10000 rpm·min−1 for 5 min), re-suspended in 1 ml PBS, and N01 and PI dyes were added according to the instructions. After 15 min of resting in a 37℃ incubator, the samples were washed twice with PBS and observed using a laser scanning confocal microscope (LMS800, ZEISS, US, Germany).

Using flow cytometry, we quantified both live and dead bacteria treated with TPP-CDs [30]. TPP-CDs of different concentrations (400 μg·mL−1 and 800 μg·mL−1) which were prepared with LB media. The suspensions of S. aureus and E. coli (1 × 108 CFU·mL−1) were subjected to centrifugation and subsequently transferred to the above TPP-CDs. After 3 h of resting in a 37 ℃ incubator, the samples were washed twice with PBS (centrifugation of 10000 rpm·min−1 for 5 min) and tested by flow cytometry (BD-C6 plus).

The effectiveness of TPP-CDs in eliminating biofilm formation was evaluated by the use of crystal violet staining [31]. A 96-well plate was inoculated with the solutions of E. coli and S. aureus (1 × 106 CFU·mL−1), 100 μL per well. The plate was placed at 37 °C for 72 h. Then the floating bacteria was removed and the biofilm was washed twice with PBS. Following this, 100 μL TPP-CDs of different concentrations (200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1) which were prepared with LB media were introduced onto a 96-well plate, 37 °C for 24 h. 200 μL methanol was added for fixing, 200 μL crystal violet (0.1 %) was added for staining. The crystal violet solution was removed. Next, the biofilm was washed three times with PBS and dissolved it by adding 200 μL 95 % ethanol. After a 15-minute period of shaking, the absorbance was measured (600 nm).

The DCFH-DA dye was used as a probe to detect the formation of ROS [32]. DCFH-DA fluorescent probes were added to S. aureus and E. coli suspensions (1 × 107 CFU·mL−1) with a final concentration of 10 μmol·mL−1. The cells which were incubated for 20 min were washed twice with PBS (centrifugation of 10000 rpm·min−1 for 5 min) and resuspended in LB media supplemented with TPP-CDs (0, 400 and 800 μg·mL−1). The cells were placed in a 37 °C incubator for 3 h and the liquid was removed by centrifugation of 10000 rpm·min−1 for 5 min. The cells were resuspended in PBS, and 10 μL of the solution was extracted to observe the fluorescence by using inverted fluorescence microscope (IX53, Olympus, Japan).

1 mL of S. Aureus and E. coli in the logarithmic growth phase were diluted to a concentration of 1 × 108 CFU·mL−1. The bacterial suspension was washed twice with PBS (centrifugation of 10000 rpm·min−1 for 5 min) and resuspended in LB media supplemented with TPP-CDs (400 μg·mL−1), at a 37 °C incubator for 2 h. The above solution was fixed by 2.5 % glutaraldehyde overnight, and dehydrated by gradient ethanol. After drying the gold spray, we examined the samples using scanning electron microscope (SEM, Regulus-8100, Hitachi, Japan)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Agarose gel electrophoresis experiments of bacterial DNA
ce:section.ce:para: 1.5 mL S. aureus and E. coli solution (1 × 108 CFU·mL−1) was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 s. Buffer RB was added and resuspended, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 s, and the supernatant was discarded. The bacteria were resuspended in 180 μL of buffer RB. 20 μL proteinase K (20 mg·mL−1) solution was added, then 200 μL liquid CB was added, at 70 ℃ for 10 min. After cooling, 100 μL isopropanol was added and mixed, transferred to an adsorption column, centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 60 s, and the waste solution was discarded. Solution was washed three times. The adsorption column was placed into a centrifuge tube, 50 μL of elution buffer EB was added, and the DNA sample was obtained by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 1 min. Samples were added to the prepared agarose gel wells at 20 μL per well for 40 min at 120 V.

Mouse mononuclear macrophage (RAW264.7 cells) in logarithmic phase were diluted to 1 × 105 CFU·mL−1, and 0.5 mL was sucked into a 24-well plate and placed in a cell incubator overnight. TPP-CDs solution (400 μg·mL−1) was prepared with media, the original media in the 24-well plate was discarded, and 0.5 mL TPP-CDs solution was sucked into the culture plate. After 12 h, fluorescence microscopy (IX53, Olympus, Japan) was used to observe at different wavelengths.

The MTT was used to test how TPP-CDs affected the survival of human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC), mouse fibroblasts (L929 cells), RAW264.7 cells, and mouse embryonic fibroblasts (NIH3T3 cells) from embryonic development. The above cells in logarithmic phase were diluted to 1 × 104 CFU·mL−1, and 0.2 mL was sucked into a 96-well plate and placed in a cell incubator overnight. After that, they were treated with TPP-CDs of different concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1600 μg·mL−1) for a period of 24 h. Following this, MTT solution was added in each well. After 4 h, the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) of 150 μL we added. After a 15-minute period of shaking, the absorbance was measured (490 nm).

The cell scratch test [33] was used to assess the migration-promoting capacity of TPP-CDs. The logarithmic phase HUVEC and L929 cells were diluted until a concentration of 2 × 105 CFU·mL−1. They were then put into 6-well plates with 2 mL in each well. After culturing the cells for 24 h, the cells of 6-well plates were vertically scraped and washed with PBS. The TPP-CDs with a concentration of 400 μg·mL−1 were prepared by using an incomplete media, and added them into a 6-well plate. The control group consisted of a media without CDs. Photographs and records were taken of the TPP-CDs solution at 0, 12, 24, and 36 h of culture. Image J software, version 1.8.0, was used to measure the beginning and ultimate area of the scratch. The shrinkage was calculated using the following formula.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA, protein extraction and inflammatory factor expression assays
ce:section.ce:para: RAW264.7 cells cultured to logarithmic growth phase (2 × 105CFU·mL−1), TPP-CDs (0, 200, and 400 μg·mL−1) and LPS (10 μg·mL−1) were mixed to RAW264.7 cells for 24 h, and three parallel groups were set. After taking out the culture media, lysate (1 mL) was mixed, then transferred to a centrifuge tube. Chloroform solution was added and vortexed for 15 s to form an emulsion. The mixture was put on ice for 5 min, after centrifugation, then an equal volume of isoacetone was added. After standing on ice for 10 min, 75 % ethanol was added to stand for 5 min, then an appropriate amount of DEPC water was added when removing ethanol. Using a Nano-100 ultramicrospectrophotometer determined the purity of samples. Then reversing transcription reaction, and each sample was repeated three times (50 °C, 15 min; 85 °C, 5 s). The relative expression levels of genes (IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β) in RAW264.7 cells induced by LPS were measured by RT-qPCR. In brief [34], ribonucleic acid (RNA) was extracted from RAW264.7 cells were reverse transcribed, screened, and studied by a Quant Studio 3 real-time PCR instrument. Table S1 indicates relevant primer sequences. Denaturation was performed at 95 °C for 30 s with 40 cycles of the reaction; melting curve (60 °C to 95 °C); and the target gene was corrected between groups according to the mRNA expression level of glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GADPH).

The same cultured cells as above were taken, the 800 μL lysate was added, transferred to a centrifuge tube, vortexed, centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min, the protein content was determined through standard curve, and the samples were added to buffer solution and boiled for 5 min to obtain protein samples. The sample and the standard product Maker were added into the prepared concentrated glue lane, 10 μL per hole, and the film was wet transferred at 100 V voltage for 120 min. After sealing, the primary antibody ((IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β) was added overnight at 4 ℃, then the second antibody was added. Finally, the results were collected by gel imaging system.

The antioxidant ability of TPP-CDs was assessed using ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging tests [35]. To summarize, DPPH and ABTS test solutions were created and then diluted to achieve an absorbance of approximately 0.7 at wavelengths of 517 nm and 734 nm. Various concentrations of TPP-CDs solutions were introduced and underwent a reaction in the absence of light at wavelengths of 517 nm and 734 nm. The clearance rate is determined by employing a specific formula.

The Animal Care and Use Committee of China Pharmaceutical University approved all animal experimental protocols (Ethics No. 2022–09-036). ICR male mice (20 g), were utilized and obtained from a supplier. It was utilized to create a model of a wound infected with bacteria. The mice were administered anesthesia using a 10 % solution of chloral hydrate. The hair on their backs was removed, and the exposed skin was cleaned with a 0.5 % iodophor solution. A circular incision measuring approximately 1 cm in diameter was made on the back using sterilized medical scissors. The incision was then treated with S. aureus bacteria (1 × 109 CFU·mL−1). Following the modeling process, the mice were allocated into three groups: the PBS control group, the TA group, and the TPP-CDs group. Each group consisted of six mice, selected at random. The wounds were cleansed using various samples, with a frequency of three times every other day, followed by the application of gauze for wrapping. The body weight was measured and tracked. On 10th day, the skin tissue with wounds from each group was preserved in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution overnight. It was then dehydrated, embedded, and stained with H&E, Masson, and immunohistochemistry (IHC) to examine the process of wound healing. On 10th day, the liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney of mice in both the TPP-CDs group and the PBS control group were retrieved for H&E staining and regular blood testing. The Nano Zoomer S60 C13210-01 slice scanner was used to capture images of all sections. The Image J software (version 1.8.0) was then utilized to analyze data such as wound size, epidermal thickness, and stained area.

The process of preparing a 2 % red blood cell suspension involved collecting fresh rat blood and subjecting it to centrifugation at a speed of 1500 rpm with 10 times the volume of PBS for a duration of 15 min. The resulting mixture was then washed 2–3 times until the supernatant no longer exhibited a red tint. The red blood cells that were recovered were processed into a 2 % solution using PBS. Various amounts of TPP-CDs were combined with a suspension of red blood cells. At the same time, negative (PBS) and positive (water) controls were prepared. After 3 h for 37 °C,we centrifuged and determined the absorbance (490 nm).

The above data were all parallel at least three times and analyzed by t-test. Data analysis was conducted using SPSS (26.0) and GraphPad Prism software. Statistical significance was determined based on the following criteria: *P<0.05, **P<0.01, and ***P<0.001.

This study employed a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize N-doped TPP-CDs using tannic acid (TA), polyethylene polyamines, and PEG1500 (Fig. 1 A). In previous work, PEGs of varying molecular weights (400, 800, 1500) were employed to prepare N-doped CDs, prioritizing biological safety. Under the conditions shown in Figure S1, the N-doped CDs made with TA, polyethylene polyamine, and PEG1500 exhibited a hemolysis ratio of less than 5 %, indicating enhanced biological safety. It is also speculated that PEG1500 may modify the structure of the synthesized CDs, potentially influencing their interaction with blood cells [36,37]. Therefore, this study selected these three components as the primary constituents for CD synthesis. Unlike the conventional dialysis method, the initial carbon dot solution was purified using the Sephadex G-50 column. When exposed to infrared light, the carbon dot solution purified with Sephadex G-50 exhibited a more pronounced Tyndall effect compared to the solution purified by dialysis, regardless of concentration. This suggests that the Sephadex G-50 column effectively separates both large and small molecules in the solution, while dialysis only separates small molecules, resulting in a more uniform solution when using Sephadex G-50. The purification time was significantly reduced from 48 h to just 1 h, greatly improving preparation efficiency (Figure S2, Table S2).

The TEM images (Fig. 1B) show that the synthesized TPP-CDs have a uniform spherical shape. Particle size analysis showed that most TPP-CDs ranged in size from 0 to 20 nm, with an average size of 9.0 nm. As depicted in Figure S3, the presence of a few larger particles was mainly attributed to nanoparticle aggregation. TPP-CDs were found to carry a negative charge in aqueous solution (Figure S4).

FTIR analysis showed a prominent absorption peak at around 3390 cm−1, as depicted in Fig. 1C. This peak indicates the presence of OH/NH hydrophilic groups at the carbon origin. This confirms that TPP-CDs dissolve well in water. The absorption peaks corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the CO, CN, CC, and CO bonds are observed at 1114 cm−1, 1348 cm−1, 1573 cm−1, and 1660 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 2943 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CH bond [21]. The elemental composition of TPP-CDs was further analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1D, the carbon dots are primarily composed of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N), with atomic percentages of 54.26 %, 26.28 %, and 19.46 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) shows the presence of three distinct peaks corresponding to CC/CC (284.80 eV), CN/CO (285.64 eV), and CO (287.00 eV). The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) shows two components: pyridinic N at 398.47 eV and pyrrolic N at 399.07 eV. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) displays three distinct peaks at 532.07 eV, 530.63 eV, and 533.37 eV, which are attributed to C-OH/COC, CO, and O-CO, respectively. TPP-CDs were analyzed under natural ambient light. The XPS results revealed a decrease in the intensity of the pyridinic N and CO peaks (Figures S5 and S6), suggesting that the generation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs led to a reduction in these electron-withdrawing groups. This indicates the potential of TPP-CDs to promote ROS production. The XPS results were found to be consistent with the FTIR data, confirming the successful synthesis of N-doped TPP-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1H, the UV–vis absorption spectra of TPP-CDs were observed to display peaks at 343 nm and 420 nm. These peaks can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the sp2 orbitals in aromatic hydrocarbons and the n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively [38]. Furthermore, when exposed to visible light, the dispersed TPP-CDs in the aqueous phase exhibit a brown coloration. The Tyndall phenomenon, indicative of uniform dispersion, is observed under infrared light, while ultraviolet light induces a blue-green coloration. To further investigate the optical properties of TPP-CDs, fluorescence spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1I, the TPP-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence with excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 nm and 462 nm, respectively, highlighting their excellent performance. Fig. 1J and 1 K show the fluorescence spectra recorded at excitation wavelengths of 300–340 nm and 350–390 nm, respectively. A slight red shift of approximately 20 nm in the emission wavelength was observed. This slight red shift suggests increased stability in the fluorescence characteristics of the TPP-CDs. The observed shift may be attributed to f orbital transitions and energy conversion within the CDs [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial performance of TPP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Wounds are highly susceptible to bacterial infection, highlighting the need for effective antibacterial agents to support wound healing. The initial step involved evaluating the in vitro antibacterial efficacy of TPP-CDs. Different concentrations of TPP-CDs were incubated with suspensions of S. aureus and E. coli, and the optical density (OD) of the mixture at 600 nm was recorded after 18 h. Figures S7 and S8 show that the mixture was clarified, with no significant bacterial growth observed at 400 μg·mL−1. TPP-CDs effectively eradicated over 90 % of S. aureus and E. coli when concentrations exceeded 400 μg·mL−1 (MIC). Bacterial passage experiments demonstrated that the MIC of TPP-CDs remained consistent after 15 passages. In contrast, amoxicillin, a commonly used antibiotic, showed changes in its MIC against S. aureus after 4 and 8 passages and against E. coli after 3 and 7 passages. The MIC of TPP-CDs against bacteria did not change (Fig. 2 G and 2H), indicating that TPP-CDs do not readily induce bacterial resistance.

Previous research studies have demonstrated that TA shows a specific antibacterial impact [39,40]. The antibacterial effects of TA and TPP-CDs were compared by plating mixtures of the two bacteria incubated with TA and TPP-CDs. The experiment revealed that, at the same dosage of 800 μg·mL−1, the TPP-CDs group had significantly fewer colonies compared to the TA group. This indicates that TPP-CDs synthesized with TA as the carbon source demonstrated enhanced antibacterial activity (Fig. 2A and 2C), which increased with higher concentrations. No colony formation was observed on the culture plate, and the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of TPP-CDs required to eradicate bacteria was also 1600 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 2B and 2D). Time inhibition curves showed that the TPP-CDs group outperformed both the TA group and the untreated control group in inhibiting the growth of S. aureus and E. coli at MIC, 2MIC, and MBC concentrations. TPP-CDs exhibited a stronger inhibitory effect on S. aureus compared to E. coli at 1/2MIC (Fig. 2E and 2F). This suggests that TPP-CDs are more effective against S. aureus than E. coli. Differences in the cell walls of S. aureus and E. coli are speculated to contribute to this disparity. The presence of pyridine N and pyrrole N in the TPP-CD structure imparts distinct surface charges. When TPP-CDs interact with the cell wall of S. aureus, the transfer of charge becomes more efficient, impacting the bacteria's regular physiological processes [23].

The live/dead bacteria were subjected to fluorescent staining, followed by qualitative analysis using laser confocal microscopy and quantitative analysis using flow cytometry. Fig. 2I and S9 shows the corresponding results. In the control group, where the bacteria were not treated with TPP-CDs, the bacteria exhibited green fluorescence, indicating 99.7 % bacterial viability. The mortality rates of S. aureus and E. coli at 400 μg·mL−1 TPP-CDs concentration exceeded 70 %, respectively. Alternatively, the presence of TPP-CDs resulted in minimal green fluorescence and mortality exceeded 90 % for both bacterial strains at 800 μg·mL−1. The reduced bacterial population, due to near-complete inhibition of bacterial growth, may explain the lower red fluorescence observed at higher concentrations compared to lower concentrations. This observation aligns with the histogram showing bacterial distribution (Fig. 2J and 2 K). Flow cytometry was used to measure bacterial numbers at a consistent rate and duration. The TPP-CDs group had a significantly lower bacterial count compared to the untreated control group under the same experimental conditions. Specifically, the bacterial count was lower in the group treated with 800 μg·mL−1 TPP-CDs compared to the group treated with 400 μg·mL−1 TPP-CDs. These results collectively demonstrate that TPP-CDs are effective in inhibiting bacterial growth and killing bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clearance of bacterial Biofilm, generation of ROS, and cleavage of bacterial DNA by TPP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Biofilm formation significantly contributes to the development of drug resistance in bacteria. Bacteria adhere to the wound surface and release proteins, polysaccharides, nucleic acids, and other substances to create a dense biofilm, which makes treatment more challenging [41,42]. CDs feature numerous functional groups on their surface, such as –COOH, –OH, CO, and –NH2. These groups enable the CDs to electrostatically adsorb onto bacterial biofilms, disrupting their structure. The biofilm-clearing efficacy of various doses of TPP-CDs against S. aureus and E. coli was quantitatively assessed using crystal violet staining. The results showed that TPP-CDs at concentrations of 200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1 resulted in reduced biofilm staining compared to the untreated bacterial group. At 800 μg·mL−1, the biofilm was barely visible under microscopy, with a clearance rate exceeding 80 % (Fig. 3 A, 3B). These findings suggest that TPP-CDs are effective as an antibacterial agent for eliminating biofilms formed by S. aureus and E. coli.

Prior research has shown that excessive ROS production by bacteria can induce oxidative stress, damaging the cell membrane and leading to bacterial death [43]. Therefore, stimulating bacteria to produce internal ROS is a key strategy for antibacterial therapy. In our study, the DCFH-DA fluorescent probe was used to evaluate the effect of TPP-CDs on ROS production in E. coli and S. aureus. Bacteria were treated with TPP-CDs at concentrations of 200, 400, and 800 μg·mL−1. Compared to the untreated groups, these bacteria exhibited increased green fluorescence and higher ROS levels (Fig. 3C and 3D). SEM images of TPP-CDs co-cultured with S. aureus and E. coli showed ruptured bacterial cell membranes (Fig. 3E). This observation supports the idea that the oxidative stress response induced by TPP-CDs can cause membrane damage and exhibit antibacterial effects. Moreover, Nucleic acids not only exist inside bacteria but also are one of the extracellular secretions that form biofilms. So we determined the effect of TPP-CDs on the DNA of S. aureus and E. coli by agarose gel electrophoresis. The results are shown in Fig. 3F. Compared with untreated bacteria, the DNA bands of S. aureus treated with TPP-CDs became weaker and the molecular weight was significantly reduced. In addition, there was no clear band in E. coli DNA treated with TPP-CDs, indicating that TPP-CDs could damage bacterial DNA and play an antibacterial role. In addition, it was found that after co-culture of TPP-CDs and RAW264.7, the cells showed blue, green, and red fluorescence at 380 nm, 440 nm, and 540 nm excitation wavelengths (Figure S10), respectively, indicating that TPP-CDs had certain penetration ability to normal cells, and it was speculated that TPP-CDs could penetrate the cells invaded by bacteria and kill bacteria.
In summary, the TPP-CDs could potentially combat bacteria by eradicating biofilm, inducing oxidative stress and cleaving bacterial DNA, thereby damaging bacterial membranes (Fig. 3G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility, promotion of cell Migration, antioxidation and regulation of inflammatory factor expression assays of TPP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HUVEC can respond to neovascularization signals, fibroblasts are the components of connective tissue, and the formation of granulation tissue with new blood vessels plays an important role in the process of wound repair. Macrophages are closely related to inflammatory factors [44]. Therefore, the compatibility of TPP-CDs with various cell types, including HUVEC cells from human umbilical veins, L929 cells from mice, RAW264.7 cells from mice, and NIH3T3 cells from humans was investigated using the MTT assay. Fig. 4 A, 4B, 4C, and S11 demonstrated that the cell survival rate remained above 90 % when treated to 1600 μg·mL−1 of TPP-CDs. It can be inferred that TPP-CDs are not toxic toward HUVEC, L929, RAW264.7, and NIH3T3.

To investigate the wound healing process, the migratory capability of HUVEC and L929 cells treated with TPP-CDs was also assessed. Compared to the control group, the scratch area on HUVEC and L929 cells treated with TPP-CDs was significantly reduced after 12 h, 24 h, and 36 h of treatment. HUVEC showed scratch area reductions of 19.58 % and 32.51 % at 12 h and 24 h, respectively, which were significantly higher than 3.59 % and 6.92 % of the control group. L929 showed reductions of 15.04 % and 38.01 % at 24 h and 36 h, respectively, which were significantly higher than 2.13 % and 6.15 % of the control group. These reductions were significantly greater than those observed in the control group (Fig. 4D, 4E, 4F, and 4G). These findings suggest that TPP-CDs possess the ability to accelerate the wound-healing process by promoting cell migration.

An optimal level of ROS is essential for maintaining normal cell viability, but excessive ROS can lead to membrane damage. Therefore, stimulating bacterial ROS generation is a key mechanism for sterilization. However, in the context of wound healing, it is crucial to eliminate excess free radicals and reduce oxidative stress [45,46]. The antioxidant capacity of TPP-CDs was assessed using ABTS and DPPH scavenging assays. In the ABTS assay, ABTS is oxidized to form the green ABTS·+ radical when exposed to oxidants, while antioxidants inhibit this reaction, reducing the green color. Absorbance was measured at 734 nm to estimate the clearance rate. Similarly, in the DPPH assay, the purple coloration of DPPH in aqueous solution changes to yellow when antioxidants neutralize the free radicals. The clearance rate was determined by measuring absorbance at 517 nm. The findings are illustrated in Fig. 4H and 4I. The scavenging ability of TPP-CDs for ABTS and DPPH free radicals is directly related to their concentration, ranging from 100 to 1000 μg·mL−1. This relationship becomes more pronounced with increasing concentrations of TPP-CDs. At 800 μg·mL−1, the free radical scavenging ability of TPP-CDs is comparable to that of ascorbic acid (AA). This suggests a potential correlation between the antioxidant potential of TPP-CDs and the presence of multiple functional groups on their surface. TPP-CDs are capable of stimulating ROS production in bacteria and eliminating excess free radicals. It is speculated that the numerous active functional groups on TPP-CDs contribute to their distinct physical and chemical properties. Specifically, the presence of CO groups may enhance the catalytic efficiency of TPP-CDs, promoting ROS production. However, under photocatalysis, CO groups can be reduced to –OH, which exhibits antioxidant properties [23,47]. As a result, TPP-CDs can induce oxidative stress in bacteria and eliminate excess free radicals at the site of injury, thereby expediting the healing process.

RT-qPCR and western blotting were used to measure the expression of three pro-inflammatory cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, in RAW264.7 induced by LPS. The results have been presented in Fig. 4J, 4 K, and 4L. This provided valuable insights into how TPP-CDs support wound healing after infections. IL-1β is a key player in many autoimmune inflammatory reactions, often initiating the release of other inflammatory mediators such as TNF-α and IL-6, which are typically highly expressed in inflamed cells [48,49]. Compared to the LPS group without TPP-CDs treatment, the protein bands for IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β were significantly weaker in the TPP-CDs groups at concentrations of 200 μg·mL−1 and 400 μg·mL−1. Quantitative analysis by PCR and Western blot also showed a significant decrease in the expression of these inflammatory factors in the TPP-CDs treatment groups.

The results indicate that TPP-CDs significantly accelerate the healing process by promoting cell migration in wounds infected with bacteria. TPP-CDs effectively inhibit the signaling of pro-inflammatory molecules within the wound. This reduction in inflammation allows cells to transition from an inflammatory state to a proliferative state, thereby speeding up the healing process (Fig. 4M). Overall, TPP-CDs prove to be a versatile therapeutic approach for enhancing wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo evaluation of infected wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the in vivo therapeutic efficacy of TPP-CDs, a mouse model of S. aureus-induced skin wound infection was established. Building on the promising in vitro findings regarding the antimicrobial and wound-healing properties of TPP-CDs (Fig. 5 A), the wounds were treated with TPP-CDs, TA, and PBS on alternate days. The wounds were then photographed and monitored over 10 days to assess healing progress. The TPP-CDs-treated wounds exhibited an accelerated healing rate, with complete wound closure and new skin tissue formation by day 8. In contrast, the TA and PBS control groups showed persistent wound defects and incomplete epithelization, as shown in Fig. 5A and 5B. On days 0, 4, and 8, wound tissue fluid was collected from each group using cotton swabs and cultured on AGAR plates. The TPP-CDs group demonstrated a significant reduction in the number of bacterial colonies compared to both the TA group and the PBS control group (Fig. 5D, 5E, and 5F). Fig. 5C shows the wound area analysis. The TPP-CDs group exhibited a reduction in the relative wound area of less than 5 % starting on the 6th day, resulting in a significantly shorter treatment duration compared to both the control and TA groups. In the early stages of wound management, prevention of bacterial infection is crucial, requiring effective antibacterial agents to eliminate germs and prevent infection. However, excessive inflammation during the later stages of wound healing can impede progress and potentially lead to complications [50,51]. Studies have shown that TPP-CDs exhibit both antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, which align with the observed improvement in wound healing in vivo. These findings suggest that TPP-CDs have considerable potential for effectively managing bacterial-infected wounds.

Wound healing is a multifaceted process involving antibacterial activity, inflammation control, and the proliferation and remodeling of cells and tissues. To evaluate the impact of TPP-CDs on wound healing, skin tissue from different groups was collected after bacterial infection and on the 10th day for hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) and Masson staining.

Fig. 5G presents the H&E staining results, which show significant inflammatory cell infiltration in the group subjected to bacterial infection, confirming the presence of an inflammatory response. After 10 days of treatment, the TPP-CDs group demonstrated reduced inflammatory infiltration, increased hair follicle formation, and enhanced angiogenesis compared to the untreated group. Furthermore, the epidermal thickness in the TPP-CDs group was significantly greater than in the other groups (Fig. 5H), indicating near-complete dermal tissue remodeling. Masson staining results (Fig. 5G and 5I) revealed that collagen fibers were more evenly distributed in the TPP-CDs group compared to the bacterial infection model group, control group, or TA monomer group. The TPP-CDs group also showed a significantly greater deposition of collagen fibers. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) was employed to quantify the levels of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and inflammatory factors IL-6 and TNF-α. Fig. 5J, 5 K, 5L, and 5 M represent the results of these analyses. In comparison to the bacterial infection model group, the TA group, and the control group, the TPP-CDs group showed significantly lower levels of IL-6 and TNF-α. Conversely, the TPP-CDs group demonstrated markedly higher levels of VEGF expression, indicating robust anti-inflammatory and pro-angiogenic properties, consistent with the findings in Section 3.4. These results highlight the efficacy of TPP-CDs in reducing inflammation, promoting collagen regeneration, and facilitating blood vessel growth when used to treat bacterially infected wounds in vivo, thereby expediting the process of skin tissue remodeling.

Ensuring optimal biocompatibility is essential for materials coming in contact with living tissues. Initially, the safety of TPP-CDs was assessed through in vitro testing. According to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) guidelines, a hemolysis rate greater than 5 % indicates significant hemolysis. The hemolysis rate of mouse red blood cells exposed to various concentrations of TPP-CDs was measured. The results indicated that while the hemolysis rate increased with higher concentrations of TPP-CDs, it remained below 5 % even at 3200 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 6 A), suggesting minimal hemolytic activity. Also, the body weight of mice in the TPP-CDs group did not differ significantly from that of the control group (Figure S12). H&E staining of primary organs (liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney) from both the TPP-CDs and control groups revealed no pathological changes or significant differences compared to the control (Fig. 6B). Routine blood tests showed that levels of hemoglobin (HGB), white blood cells (WBC), platelets (PLT), and neutrophils (Gran#) in the TPP-CDs group were comparable to those in the control group, all within normal reference ranges (Fig. 6C, 6D, 6E, and 6F). These findings indicate that TPP-CDs exhibit favorable biocompatibility, supporting their potential for safe use in managing bacterial-infected wounds.

In summary, TPP-CDs were successfully synthesized, and their performance was assessed across multiple dimensions. Characterization confirmed the consistency in morphology, abundance of functional groups, and favorable optical properties of TPP-CDs. Efficacy studies demonstrated that TPP-CDs effectively destroy bacterial biofilms and DNA, induce ROS production in bacteria, and exhibit robust antibacterial activity without detectable resistance. Furthermore, TPP-CDs facilitate wound healing by clearing excess free radicals, stimulating cell proliferation, inhibiting inflammatory factors such as IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α, and promoting hair follicle development, angiogenesis, and collagen fiber deposition. TPP-CDs also possess cell imaging capabilities. Compared with previous studies on positively charged antibacterial CDs doped with metal combined with photothermal therapy [20,21,27,28], these N-doped, negatively charged TPP-CDs, free from metal doping, demonstrate high biological safety and more antibacterial and anti-inflammatory mechanisms without the need for additional adjuvant therapies. This results in a streamlined, one-stop treatment strategy. And the purification process was improved using Sephadex G-50 column chromatography instead of traditional dialysis, enhancing preparation efficiency. Overall, this one-stop N-doped negatively charged TPP-CDs nanoplatform shows considerable potential and application prospects in the treatment of bacterial infections.
